speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is Friday, May, 5, 1995. The survivor is Eddie Ilan. Interviewer, Leonia Kurgan. City, Los Angeles, California, United States of America. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Dr. Leonia Kurgan, and I am interviewing Eddie Ilan today. And I spell my name L-E-O-N-I-A. Last name, K-U-R-G-A-N. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] So tell me your name now.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Eddie Ilan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eignatz Brzezowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-R-Z-E-Z-O-W-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the name of the town where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sochaczew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- S. If I can remember. S-C-H-O-C-Z-E-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me what your birth date was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think I was born on 7/14/1928. My sister says that I was born in '29. So I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how are you now? How old do you consider yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I consider myself 67.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Tell me a little bit about your life before the war. What was your everyday life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in this little town. I know now how far it is from Warsaw. At that time I didn't know. It's 53 kilometers west of Warsaw. And not too many Jews lived there. I'd say a couple of thousand. Because I don't remember at that time how many were there. I went to school, I went to cheder, and I went to yavneh. And I also went, later on to [POLISH], which is the regular school. There were two Jews in the school, me and somebody else. And everyday life--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there antisemitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was-- yes, there was. We-- right across from where we lived there was like a shopping center. And there was, every Friday when the farmers in the villages came to this little village to shop for the weekend, there was a sign out there that was scribbled on, [NON-ENGLISH] which means, if you buy from a Jew, you're a dog. And that was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [? paper, ?] as I remember. I got beat up many a times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who got beat up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got beat up many of times just-- just because, when I went to [POLISH], they would call me Zyd. And that was an accepted part-- an accepted part of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well for one thing, the teachers didn't hide it, the school didn't hide it, and I didn't hide it. In order to belong someplace and to defend myself, so to speak, I even joined [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was like a family you belonged to. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- what should I tell you? I remember my parents. My father was very orthodox. He was a shoemaker. Where did he have his shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: No shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in-- in this little town on [? Varshavska ?] Street, number 15. I don't remember. My sister told me, and I believe her. I do remember the room that we lived in. There were five of us, one room. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess I was the only poor one in Europe. I don't know. But we were poor. And my mother used to-- used to cook for the farmers-- the villagers-- that came to the city on Friday-- on Tuesday and Friday. And I would take off from school and go and carry the food out to them to make a zloty. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Once a month, on the first of the month, there was like a big market. It came from far-- further away. And I would take a bucket of soda pops, called [? kvass, ?] and go around between the horses and the cows as they were selling them to give to the people and make a half of dollar-- a zloty, rather.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [? kvass ?] was like a cool drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: A soda pop.

speaker: interviewer
text: A soda pop, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: A soda pop. And that I would do once a month. That I do remember. And I remember one policeman. His name was Albert. That I do remember. And the only thing he knew was how to beat up Jewish kids. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When I went back this time, in 1993, and asked the curator of the museum if he knew about Albert, he's the only one that remembered him. And he said, oh, Albert the cow? He says, I remember him. I said, do you remember how he used to beat up the Jewish kids? He says, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this man should remember. He was older than I am. He was in his late 80s in 1993, so he should remember. But I guess, like all of them, they don't. Or they don't want to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your sisters speaking up as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had three sisters. And most of them-- well, all three of them-- are bigger than I am. My oldest sister is now living in Israel. My middle sister was killed in Poland for a bottle of vodka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was an edict in Poland at that time after the War to-- for a liter of vodka, kill a Jew or bring him in alive. Well, she was killed. And my small sister, my little sister, she was in the Warsaw ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And now I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's about all I can tell you about this little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't remember if they were beaten up or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too small even to-- I was just--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, according to--

speaker: interviewer
text: According to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: According to me, I was 10 years old when the war broke out. According to my sister, I was nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't-- it's very hard. It's-- when I went back now, everything was so big, huge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The town hasn't changed a bit. But it was big, because at that-- at that time. Now in two steps I cross the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a big difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do as a family? Do you remember? Before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only thing I can remember, actually, is going to shul, going to cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a four corner building. In one of the rooms was, like, a prayer house called a shtibl. But the shul was, at that time, a long ways away. But actually, it wasn't. It was only four blocks away.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't go there very often. You went to the shtibl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just on holidays we went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: On high holiday, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. What did you wear when you went to the shtibl? Did you dress differently, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Honest, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to out eat. See, I don't remember if I ever had. The only thing I remember is my mother saying, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's better to have a patch on top of a patch [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] then to have a torn spot. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That I remember. [CRYING] I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she took good care of the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I guess so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: With what she had. I guess so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you ate? What meals were like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember Passover we would get some matzohs from-- from America. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember on Friday she would buy the little fishes that were maybe two inches long, and skin them and chop them for hours until they were nice and chopped up so we could have fish for Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she make gefilte fish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Out of those little fish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the little fish called? Do you remember? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gefilte? Gefilte fish?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the actual fish. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember. They were not bigger than a finger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Did she do that herself, or did your sisters help her, or did you help her?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters went to school, so I don't-- I really don't remember. All I know is that Friday night we had gefilte fish. And I remember there was one gentile, a farmer, who came to town about once a month. And he, like of, adopted me. He wanted me to be his god-- his godson. Because when I was born, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] according to my parents, according to him, he came in and bought a pair of shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the moment of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I remember. They told me. So I remembered him coming. And I had to take off from school, be in the house, and as far as I can remember, I had to drink a shot of vodka with him. That was expected and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you make of that? What did you think of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just thought that was a nice man who deserved to be nice to. But as a kid, I didn't pay any attention to all of that. All I know is he came in, I jumped on his knee, and he gave me five groszy to go and buy a ice cream. That was-- it was called a lody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lody. Yes. Lody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lody. Yeah. And after I had my drink of vodka, I went out and played and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: He must've liked you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I wish I would.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had, in this four square building, there were mostly Jews living there. The friends that I had were mainly from school. And we played soccer. And we were too young to go to the cinema. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had two of them in this little town. But we would hang out in the little market. We thought it was big, and now it's small. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my sisters had friends. They used to come into the house. But that's about all I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you closest to anyone in particular in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was the smallest, and I was a boy. So I was considered the Kaddish. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They tell me-- told me-- my sister still is telling me-- that I was treated the best of them all because--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was the Kaddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that mean that you would say a prayer?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- when they die, I would--

speaker: interviewer
text: When the die, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought I-- I thought I-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So you didn't feel that you were treated in any special way.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I must have. I must have. I was taken to shul. My sisters were not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took you to shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like going to shul with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked it because there were kids there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We played, we jumped around. I remember every one of us had payot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Payot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: So that kind of marked you to be very-- as a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes. What did you wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just regular clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Regular clothes. Yes. What kind of shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father used to make a pair of shoes that had to last four years. So the first year they were real big and then we put some cotton into them so I can fit in. And then every year, he would take out the little cotton. So by the time the shoes were worn out, my foot fit in it perfect. That was a way of doing it, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he make good shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. People came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What dream did you have for yourself? Did you have any dream of what you would be or what would you do with your life as a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a way of life, and I didn't think I was going anyplace, do anything. Because I didn't think. It was not a part of to think. It was just to go to school, come home, eat, go out and play. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess I must have. I didn't run away. I must have been happy. My family was happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see your mother and father as happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: With each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We lived in-- I could have-- couldn't have lived there without seeing everything. We lived in a one room, five people. So I had to see it. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw a lot of your parents and their life together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until today, I can't figure out how five people can live in one room and have three kids. I don't-- I can't understand it. But I guess when you live in that kind of life, everything comes out fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was there a kitchen, or where did your mother cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a little place on the side as you walked into the door. There was a stove for wood. We had-- we had no refrigerator, so-- but there was a piece of the door that was cut out. A piece of wood like a hole. So we can put the food there so we can keep it cool, because Poland is cold all the time. And we had two beds, and at night, the table was moved and another cot was-- a bed was opened up for the two-- for the two big sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they slept in one bed, and you slept by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I slept with my father, and my little sister slept with my mother. And my other two sisters slept together.

speaker: interviewer
text: In another. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: In another one. I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before winter, the-- the farmer would come and unload potatoes and we would put them under the bed. We had no place else, I guess. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I also remember, Friday we would take a pot of potatoes, [? cold chilled, ?] and take it to the bakery, which was in the same four buildings. And Saturday after shul we would go and take it. And we would eat it for lunch, and then put the pot under the [NON-ENGLISH], under the blankets to keep it warm so we can have it later, because you could not have a fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: No fire in Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I also remember there was a big party in the building when we got lights.

speaker: interviewer
text: Electricity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Electricity. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had [? lafta. ?] That I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had what?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Lafta. ?] Lamps.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Lafta. ?] Before the electricity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then we got one bulb in. It was like a God sent light.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year that was? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember. But it was before the war, because I remember getting it. I remember. But I don't remember which year. Years to me at that time was nothing. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a little boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's go out and play soccer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Soccer, the same as they play here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same soccer. Yes. And was it all Jewish boys playing, or mixed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where we lived, all Jewish boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: All Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We could not, or would not, go and play with the other boys because they would just beat the things out of you and-- so we just decided not to go and play with them. They didn't come here. We didn't go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell your parents that you were beaten up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many a times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did-- what-- what-- what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn to fight? Or how did you protect yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's why--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's why I joined [NON-ENGLISH] so that when-- I don't remember how long it was, how it was before the war. I mean, how many years. But I must have been a baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, a little boy. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when we went out, there were a bunch of us who would never go alone. Like, even to walk down the street from one corner to the other, we would go as a bunch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn to protect yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to. But you couldn't fight because, if you fought, the cops would come and take you away and-- and beat you up, this Albert. We had only--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish kids they would take away and beat them up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was only one policeman and he was the worst there ever was. So we learned, but we couldn't. We couldn't fight back. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you dealt with it the best you could.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Do you remember what they said to you when you told them stories like that? What were their words?

speaker: interviewee
text: To whom?

speaker: interviewer
text: To your parents. What did they say? You don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: What they said when I told them I was beaten up?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They never said fight back. They just said, next time, run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next time run?

speaker: interviewee
text: Run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't get into a fight. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But that was not only me. It was a way of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know you're going to go out, you're going to go for a walk, you're going to get hit, you're going to get kicked, you're going to be spat on, and you're going to be called Zyd. And I guess maybe it was an identification that I was a Zyd. But I believed that I was a Zyd. I am a Zyd. So that was part of life I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear any stories about that being a Zyd, being-- having a culture, and a background? And did you ever learn about--

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, about anything special about Jews? Did anybody tell you stories or?

speaker: interviewee
text: We wouldn't talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents wouldn't talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: About the heritage, the culture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they would say, we are Jewish, we are Orthodox. We are religious. We believe in God. And when you go to cheder, they teach you things. But it was-- my parents, I guess they-- now I know they never philosophised. They never said anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they read?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had to read. They-- they were there reading in Hebrew, reading the Bible. My father-- I don't know if he went to [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know. I don't know if my mother did, but I know my sisters did. My mother had a fixation I guess-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that you must learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: You must learn. She put importance on that? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No matter what, she says, you must learn. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If it's Jewish, or Hebrew, or Polish-- learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Did I learn? What did I learn?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you read?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do I read Polish?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, did you read as a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I read Polish. I wrote Polish, Yiddish, Hebrew. I could [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] say the morning prayers by heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hebrew. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I could say-- I could speak a little Hebrew. I could say evening prayers almost by heart, I remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I spoke Polish pretty good. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I still do. But I don't speak it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think I should. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I should not categorize it as all of Poland, but most of the Polish people don't deserve to have me speak their language. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But that's my feeling. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I learn some from the Polish? Yes I did. I learned [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that if you hate, you're in trouble. You're very nasty. So I learned not to hate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But up to a point, I can't forgive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I guess I'll die with it, that I can't forgive certain things that shouldn't have been done to me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't do anything to them. I was a little baby. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How bad could I have been at 9, 10 years old? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I guess that's life. Do you remember what happened when war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The day that war broke out? The 1st of September?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I stop? I forgot to signal the--
